narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated an 83.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. You have a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50, which is above the typical 3.21 average, and an LSAT score of 38, higher than the usual 36.70—those are genuine strengths that many applicants would envy. However, your decile rankings in school, particularly your Year 3 position at the bottom of your class compared to the middle-of-the-pack average, stood out as the single most influential factor pushing the model toward predicting failure. Right behind that was your similarly low Year 1 decile, making early academic struggles the top two drivers in this tough call. Your upper-middle family income level, while comfortable, also nudged the prediction toward failure more than average cases, followed by your LSAT, undergraduate GPA, racial background as White, gender as male, and full-time enrollment status—all contributing in descending order of influence, though none strongly countered the overall risk. Compared to typical students, your demographics align closely with the majority—White and male students make up most of the dataset, and full-time status is the norm—so they didn't help or hurt much beyond the baseline. These low decile rankings signal to the model a higher risk of bar struggles, even with your stronger test scores and GPA, as they've historically correlated with first-try failures in similar profiles. It's a narrow lens, but it prioritizes early academic signals over later achievements. Looking ahead, you can't change your past deciles, LSAT, GPA, income, or demographics, but you can realistically boost your bar prep by targeting weaknesses those low rankings might hint at, like building consistent study habits or seeking tutoring in core subjects. Retaking the LSAT for an even higher score could help future applications, as it remains a key factor. Enrolling part-time if it allows more focus time might subtly shift perceptions, though full-time is common. Ultimately, dedicated bar exam preparation courses have turned around predictions for students in your shoes—consider starting there to prove the model wrong and reapply stronger.
